steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage asserts that the conversion of forest to cattle pasture during the 1960s and 1970s is "evident in" the graph data. The correct choice must point to graph data from that specific period that shows a decline in forest patches, which is what deforestation looks like.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'high number of patches in class VII and Class VIII in 1986.': 1986 is after the cattle boom ended (1960s-1970s), so this data point cannot show the conversion period. ✗
  • Choice B — 'difference between the number of patches in class VII and in Class VI in 2000.': 2000 is decades after the conversion period. A cross-class comparison at that date shows nothing about the 1960s-1970s deforestation. ✗
  • Choice C — 'increase in the number of patches for all classes from 1979 to 2000.': An increase in forest patches means forests are recovering or expanding, which is the opposite of deforestation. This contradicts the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — 'decrease in the number of patches for all classes from 1960 to 1979.': A decrease in forest patches across all classes from 1960 to 1979 maps exactly onto the conversion period the passage describes. Fewer patches means forests were being cleared for pasture. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
